title: Entanglement entropy of an acoustic black hole
authors: P. C. van de Graaf, H. T. C. Stoof
abstract: We introduce a method to numerically compute the entanglement entropy of an acoustic black hole. It is shown that the entanglement entropy of sufficiently large subregions scales linearly with size and thus shows a volume law instead of an area law. The origin of this scaling can be traced back to the non-separable long-distance correlations due to the production of phonon pairs at the horizon. The system is shown to be locally thermal, such that the part of the entanglement entropy scaling with volume is well approximated by the thermal entropy of the outgoing Hawking radiation.
summary: This work develops a method to calculate the entanglement entropy for an acoustic black hole analogue in a Bose-Einstein condensate. The authors demonstrate that due to long-distance correlations induced by the horizon, the entanglement entropy scales with volume rather than the expected area law. This result connects condensed matter physics phenomena to fundamental concepts in quantum gravity.
main_problem: The paper computes the entanglement entropy of an acoustic black hole analogue system.
main_result: The entanglement entropy exhibits a volume law scaling far from the horizon, which is well approximated by the local thermal entropy of the outgoing Hawking radiation.
method: The calculation uses the correlator method for Gaussian states, involving mode-function decomposition and solving Bogoliubov-de Gennes equations.
model_or_system: The system is an acoustic black hole realized in a superfluid Bose-Einstein condensate, governed by fluctuations described by a modified Heisenberg equation.
key_observables: Entanglement entropy ($S_E$), density-density correlation function, and outgoing phonon occupation numbers.
important_parameters: The local healing length ($\xi$) acts as the natural Planck scale; the analysis depends on the transsonic flow regime at the horizon.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on Bogoliubov theory and assumes the system can be treated as a continuum or a tight-binding model approximation.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the acoustic black hole setup, dispersion relations showing supersonic/subsonic regions, and plots comparing entanglement entropy scaling (logarithmic vs. volume law) against subsystem size.
paper_structure: The paper develops the theory by first setting up the Hamiltonian and solving the Bogoliubov equations, then calculating particle creation via scattering theory, and finally applying the correlator method to derive the entanglement entropy scaling laws.
why_interesting: It provides a concrete, solvable analogue system (BEC) to study fundamental concepts from quantum gravity, such as Hawking radiation and entanglement entropy scaling laws (area vs. volume law).
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 2: The dispersion relation of the continuous Bogoliubov theory in the supersonic (left), and the subsonic (right) regions. Positive and negative normed branches are colored green and red respectively. The momenta are labeled by i/o referring to ingoing and outgoing modes respectively, and ± to differentiate between positive and negative normed solutions. The flow profile has cl/|v| = 1/2 and cr/|v| = 3/2.

Figure 3: The dispersion relation of the modified Bogoliubov theory in the supersonic (left), and the subsonic (right) regions for positive frequencies. Positive and negative normed branches are colored green and red, respectively. The momenta are labeled by i, o referring to ingoing and outgoing modes respectively, and ± to differentiate between positive and negative normed solutions. Different matching regimes are labeled by the roman numerals I to IV. The flow profile has cl/|v| = 1/2 and cr/|v| = 3/2. For visibility, a relatively large mass parameter ω0a/cl = 0.001 and small healing length ξl = 0.1a are used.

Figure 4: The occupation numbers of outgoing Hawking pairs in the supersonic (left) and subsonic (right) regions as a function of frequency for the tight-binding model with gap ω0a/cl = 5 · 10−5, and healing length ξl = 4a. The flow profile is chosen such that cl/|v| = 1/2 and cr/|v| = 3/2. The subsonic region is compared to the analytical results for the continuous theory found by Ref. [25].

Figure 5: The density-density correlation function of the analogue black hole. The flow profile has cl/|v| = 1/2 and cr/|v| = 3/2, with the healing length ξl = a and gap ω0a/cl = 10−4. In order to see the long-distance correlations clearly, the short-distance correlations (diagonal) are masked. Dashed white lines correspond to the peaks as described by Eq. (29), and are labeled by the Greek letters α, β and γ. The horizontal dashed line denotes the cut corresponding to Fig. 10.
Figure 6: The entanglement entropy of a finite-size homogeneous Bogoliubov theory for ξ = 0.01a and gap ω0a/cr = 10−8, compared to the exact results from conformal field theory.
